Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I'd try to set aside time each day to read through materials, but with everything going on, it rarely worked out that way. I ended up doing a lot of late-night cramming sessions, especially before exams. I'd print out readings or download PDFs and study them away from my computer because screens were adding to my stress. My routine was more reactive than planned, but I made it work somehow.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how scared I was about falling behind. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, and then other weeks I'd be up until 3 AM trying to catch up. Deadlines definitely drove my schedule more than any personal routine. I kept telling myself I'd get more consistent, but the anxiety made it hard to stick to a plan. When I did study regularly, it was usually because a peer study group kept me accountable.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I got an email notification about something due or when someone in my peer group mentioned an upcoming deadline. Fear of missing something important was honestly my main motivator. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements I'd missed, even if I didn't plan to do actual work. The anxiety of not knowing what was happening in the course pushed me to check in, even when I didn't feel ready to engage.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester when logging in felt more routine, mostly because my peer support group would meet online and we'd go through the LMS together. Having that accountability made it feel less scary. But that routine fell apart when things got really stressful and I started avoiding everything. It never became truly automatic for me—it always required effort to push myself to log in.
Q5: The discussion boards were actually really helpful because I could see how other students were thinking about the material. Reading their posts made me feel less alone and sometimes clarified concepts I was struggling with. I also found the reading materials useful once I downloaded them and could annotate them in my own way. The peer interactions on the forums were probably what kept me connected to the course when I felt like giving up.
Q6: I barely watched the videos, to be honest. I'd start them but my attention would drift, and the length made them feel overwhelming. I kept telling myself I'd go back to them but rarely did. Some of the optional supplementary readings I ignored completely because I was already drowning in the required stuff. When you're scared of failing, you focus on what seems most directly tied to your grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exam or if it helped reduce my anxiety about the material. Anything that made concepts clearer or that I could discuss with my peer group felt valuable. If something seemed abstract or disconnected from assessments, I'd skip it because I didn't have the mental energy to spare. Practicality and stress reduction were my main criteria.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're already behind on required work and feeling scared about your performance, optional stuff just adds to the guilt. I'd see them listed and think 'maybe later,' but later never came. Required activities had urgency attached to them, which actually helped me prioritize even when I was struggling.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with printed notes and PDFs I'd downloaded. I'd take them to the library or study with my peer group in person. I also used external YouTube videos to understand concepts better because sometimes a different explanation clicked. My textbook got way more use than the LMS would ever show.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was studying intensively but my LMS activity was minimal. I'd downloaded everything I needed early in the week and then worked through it on paper. My exam prep was almost entirely offline—I made flashcards, wrote summaries by hand, and quizzed myself with friends. The LMS would show I was absent, but I was actually working harder than ever.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a reading or lecture slides once, download them immediately, and then never open them on the LMS again. Studying offline felt safer somehow, like I could control my environment better. It also meant I wasn't distracted by notifications or the anxiety of seeing how much else I hadn't done yet.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially around midterms when everything piled up at once. I felt paralyzed by fear of failure and would avoid logging in because seeing my incomplete tasks made the anxiety worse. My attendance dropped because I couldn't face the pressure. The peer support intervention actually helped pull me back, but there were dark weeks where engaging felt impossible.
Q13: Fatigue was a huge factor. When I was exhausted and stressed, the LMS felt like this overwhelming mountain I couldn't climb. I'd log in, see everything I needed to do, and just freeze. Competing responsibilities like my extracurriculars and family stuff also pulled my attention away. Sometimes I'd choose to do something else just to feel competent at something, even though I knew I should be studying.
Q14: Many times. I'd open readings and scroll through them without actually absorbing anything because my mind was racing with worry. Or I'd watch part of a video while thinking about everything else I needed to do. Being physically present on the LMS didn't mean I was mentally there. My engagement was often superficial when my anxiety was peaking.
Q15: I avoided assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The fear of doing them badly made me put them off, which only made the fear worse. It's a terrible cycle. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first, but really I was just scared. My assignment completion rate shows this—I submitted way less than I should have.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I'd drift and avoid. When a deadline approached, the fear would spike enough to overcome my avoidance. I'd often submit things at the last minute or just past the deadline. The structure of having due dates was stressful but necessary for me to function at all.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag. Good feedback motivated me to try harder, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to hide. After getting peer support, I got better at using feedback constructively. But early in the semester, any negative feedback would send me spiraling and make me avoid that type of task. It took time to develop thicker skin.
Q18: The course structure was pretty demanding, which added to my stress. Having so many online components meant there was always something I could be doing, which sounds good but actually increased my anxiety. The clear deadlines helped, but the volume of content was overwhelming. I wish there had been more built-in checkpoints or scaffolding to help pace things out.
Q19: The discussion forums made engagement easier because they felt more human and less isolating. But the sheer amount of video content made things harder—I couldn't keep up with all of it. Having peer interaction opportunities built into the course helped a lot. What made it harder was when everything felt equally urgent and I couldn't tell what to prioritize.
Q20: I think that describes someone like me earlier in the semester—logging in a lot out of anxiety, clicking around, starting things but not finishing them. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just spinning your wheels, paralyzed by fear. Or you're searching for something that will make the material click but not actually producing work.
Q21: This is basically my story with exams. I studied intensively offline, understood the material deeply through my own methods, and performed well on tests. But my LMS activity was low because my real learning happened elsewhere. The system can't see you sitting with a textbook for hours or discussing concepts with friends. Performance and logged activity don't always correlate.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're anxious-scrolling or deeply focused. They miss offline study completely. They also can't capture the emotional journey—the fear, the breakthroughs, the peer conversations that happen outside the platform. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're thinking about course concepts even when you're not studying, or when you're excited to discuss ideas with peers. For me, engagement was often mixed with fear, but underneath that fear was genuine investment in learning. It's about mental and emotional presence, not just clicking buttons.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group meant to my success, or how many hours I spent studying offline with printed materials. It wouldn't capture the nights I lay awake worried about failing, or the moment things finally clicked during a conversation with a classmate. It can't show resilience—how I kept going despite being scared. My grade shows I succeeded, but the data misses the struggle and the human connections that got me there.
